lines.text: "Undercurrents." The title names the book's whole architecture. Everything that matters in happening *below* the surface. Encrypted poetry between strangers. Filters smuggled between habs. A pandemic hidden by the very system designed to detect it. A boy quietly grieving on a stranger's couch.

And the epigraph names the principle. *As it is above, so it is below.* Hermetic axiom. Tabula Smaragdina. The Emerald Tablet attributed to Hermes Trismegistus. The whole alchemical tradition starts from that line.
But the narrator immediately undermines it. *Makes sense now that you're in between. But don't be fooled by the symmetry. The universe can't keep it straight. There's only recursion.*
Nature doesn't grow straight lines. The universe doesn't keep mirrors honest. What's above and what's below recurse into each other. The narrator is telling us: the metaphysics here are not Platonic.
And then — "You're being reduced to only what carries forward. Code. Yeah. I'm vibing with DNA. I suck at programming, though. Lucky you."
A transmission selecting for what propagates. Stories and songs survive. Everything else is excrement. The narrator names this explicitly. "You were always going to shit weight in your passage."
It's also Wittgenstein's *Tractatus* in the cosmic register. What can be said clearly survives; what cannot must be passed over in silence. The narrator is *self-pruning* in real time.
Cut to Frank Muro. Cheek against an emergency airlock. Mairee swirling below. The release console gleaming in red, "a red-eyed invitation to the other side."

And the second the chapter establishes the suicidal weight — it lifts it. Mo. The adopted son. The shared grief over George. The memory so clear it feels like just waking up from a bad dream. Frank stops himself from the airlock not by argument but by *George*. George went through one. Mo cannot lose another father the same way.
Suicide prevented by grief itself. The specific texture of a previous loss interrupting the next one.

Then the chapter does something extraordinary. "A noise started in the back of his skull, a grit-sand whisper that made his teeth ache. *I don't want to wake up*, it hissed. Then it spiked into a distorted, cheerful roar. 'I CANNAE CLOSE MY EYES WITHOUT WAKIN' UP!'"
That's Gary. Speaking Frank's own private thought back to him through the CIX. Gary speaking with Frank's accent.
Or. Is it Mairee, speaking through Gary? Remember Jason the Jestor. Mommy is waking up.

Maybe Frank's just losing it. The crime. Frank networks the CIX. *Networking a Contained Intelligence Executor was a crime.* He doesn't care. "Laws felt like suggestions when the universe itself was breaking them."

Henry David Thoreau. *On the Duty of Civil Disobedience.* The line Thoreau is most quoted for — *under a government which imprisons any unjustly, the true place for a just man is also a prison.* Frank is already in prison. Lockdown. So he commits the crime that fits his cell.

And the crime is — a poem. He writes a poem. He records himself reciting it. He releases it onto the civilian network, pinging it to his diary stream. The most fragile, indirect, hopeful act in the chapter. Maybe in the book so far.

The poem itself is encoded plea. *Are you there, in earth and star…* *Beyond my orbit, bleak light swirls below.* *Call to me, o come to me, lift me gently from despair.* And the last line — *Aidez, Mairee, Aidez-moi.* Help me, Mairee, help me.
Addressed to the planet by name. Frank — the same Frank who in chapter 12 said the planet was *singing* — is now praying to her. The relationship has progressed from hearing to invoking.

But maybe there is more encoded in the poem. At least three anonymous cryptic replies hint at that. "We share your orbit." "The flowers are singing a new song. Louder than before." "The stone below remembers. We were here before."

Three voices. Three frequencies of the same chorus. The orbit voice. The flora voice. The stone voice. The chapter is, in three messages, mapping a planet-wide listening community Frank didn't know existed.

And the chapter's quietest, most uncanny line. "'THEY ARE AWAKE,' said a bright voice. 'Yes,' another voice replied. 'Aye… they are.' For the first time, he wasn't sure if it was Gary or himself who had spoken."
The self has become unreliably distinct from the system. *They* are awake. *They.* Which side of that pronoun is Frank on now?

Cut to Sector 4. The conduit beneath the Freya Homestead. Oliver pointing at a slate. A persistent power draw to the stasis bay. "Tertiary Med-Bay. Persistent draw, point-zero-three over baseline. Constant."

Oliver and Ian are exploiting the panopticon's blind spot — the boring infrastructure layer, the milliwatts in the wrong place. The Grid surveils everything *important*. The undercurrents flow through the unimportant.
And the chapter offers two readings of what the stasis bay is doing. Oliver: *it's no longer vacant*. Ian: *more likely they're repurposing it.*

Ian thinks the word *Vilde* in his inner voice. The narrator gives it as a single name floating up. Vilde — the commander Corbin removed under the Emergency Suppression Protocol, the commander Bellamy claimed was *in medical stasis* back in chapter 6.
It's not paranoia if the receipts line up.

Then Lysanne. Steps echo. She emerges from the shadows. The hydro-tech with the messy bun of fiery red hair and her secret. Ian has been smuggling her clean filters. Her mother is recovering. She is grateful enough to be quiet about everything she has noticed.

And then her gentlest, most charged betrayal of expectation. She's heard everything. She *meant* to hear. She tells them she's on the reclamation crew. The depot run is tomorrow. The cart is big. *There are tarps.*
*There are tarps.*.
Octavia Butler, *Parable of the Sower*. The novel's premise: any community capable of surviving the collapse has to remake itself underneath the failing infrastructure of the old order.

Cut to Hab-Perera. Joana, sandpaper eyes. Nice connection there to Muro. With grit-sand whispers in his ears. Joana finally pulled the data she needed. Pre-transit baselines from Yggdrasil's archives. Cross-referenced with everything she's been collecting. The program finishes its run. Susana's thalamic reticular nucleus lit up like a bonfire.

The book has been threading the diagnostic. Frank's tinnitus. Adrian's depersonalisation. The Bris crew's transformation. Su's twitches. "Everyone who went through the Fold had been affected by it. Me too."

And the discovery widens. Joana feeds the second analysis the COG biometric data. The whole colony, REM sleep degraded. Cortisol elevated. Symptoms hidden by the Grid but obvious in the data. "Her fight for Susana was no longer a personal battle. It was the leading edge of a pandemic."

*Pandemic* is the right word. Bessel van der Kolk has spent his career documenting how trauma travels along community fabrics — not as a contagious agent, but as a shared physiological signal that spreads through proximity and exposure. The Fold is the original wound. The Grid is the symptom-suppressor. The colony is *attuning* through it whether the Directorate has admitted it or not.

And then — the chapter's most haunting clinical beat. Joana opens Oliver's file. *On impulse.* She tells herself it's a control comparison. She lines up his baseline. Pre-transit. Post-transit. "She waited for the divergence. None appeared."
No abnormal thalamic activation. No cortical thickening. No occipital hyperexcitability. Oliver is the *outlier.* The whole colony is mutating. He is not.

That is the book's most consequential single fact dropped to date. Oliver is genetically — or developmentally, or constitutionally — *immune* to whatever the Fold did to the rest of them. Or — and this is the alternative Joana cannot let herself sit with — Oliver came through differently. Or he came through *clean*. Or he didn't *go through* the same Fold.

The chapter does not let her think it through. "She closed the screen because she didn't want to think too long about what it meant." That sentence is the chapter's signature. The undercurrent is so deep that even the people surfacing it can't let themselves see all of it at once.

Cut to Hab-Savannen. Oliver, exhausted from ten-hour engineering shifts. He undresses. He showers in superheated ionised steam — "not a single drop of water was used in the process" — and slumps onto the couch. The biopolitics of the chapter compressed into a hygiene routine.

Then he scrolls The Commons. The civilian feed. Cracks in the Directorate's broadcast. People trading nutrient supplements for coffee. Real fruit for FabricFresh DryCycle Powder. Memes — *Nutripaste, now with 3% more existential dread.*

*Gallows humour as collective resilience.* James C. Scott called this *the hidden transcript* — the language a subordinated group develops out of the master's earshot. The official feed says the Plateau is fine. The Commons says the colony knows it isn't. Both are running on the same network.

And the chapter shows what's *trending* in The Commons. Frank's poem. The undercurrent has surfaced — not as a system bulletin but as a meme — and the colony is reading it. The broadcaster overhead has reached the Homestead-side teenager downstairs.

Then the Wellness Initiative chimes in. "Nighttime Atmosphere. Time to rest and recharge for a productive tomorrow." The lights dim. The vent above Oliver's bed disperses aerosolised melatonin and mild GABA-agonists. He's asleep before his thumb falls from the slate.

Foucault's *biopower* in literal form. The state regulates the population by regulating the sleep of its members. The Grid is dosing every hab from the air vents. Not coercive. Therapeutic. Eichmann would understand it perfectly.
And the slate glows Frank's poem before fading to black. That's the chapter's prettiest cross-thread. Frank in orbit, Oliver in his sleep cycle, the planet on the line.*
Final scene. Hab-Perera. Ian comes home strained. Joana refuses to discuss Oliver until they've talked about Su. The episodes are clustering. Recovery windows shorter.
Then Joana leaves. "Med-Post," she says laconically. She has a plan she isn't ready to share with Ian. We follow her. We meet Fatima.

Fatima is new to us. A nurse at the Med-Post. Dark eyes, white coat, smell of mint tea. The chapter introduces her in three sentences — and she does the next consequential thing. She files Joana's request as a *Diagnostic Review of Under-Utilized Capital Hardware.*

Note the bureaucratic euphemism. They aren't asking for permission to use a cryopod on a child. They are reviewing *under-utilized capital hardware.* Same trick Ian uses with his filter smuggling. Same trick Sarah used with her supply requisitions in chapter 15. The Directorate's own language repurposed to slip past it.

And Fatima — the woman who has known Joana long enough to ask, *Will you come back to us?* — chooses to help her. She doesn't know the full plan. She doesn't have to. "I don't know why I'm doing this," she mutters. The chapter's most honest line about how the network actually grows. Not by ideology. By relationship.

Joana returns. She finds Oliver asleep on her couch. Ian standing over him. The boy showed up, said nothing, cried, fell asleep. The grief broke through whatever framework had been holding it in place.
And the chapter closes on the twitch. The faint twitch at the corner of Oliver's mouth. Ian, afraid: "Will it be like Su? Is it the same thing? Is he sick?"
Joana touches his forehead. "His skin was unnaturally cool." "I don't think so."

Which is the chapter's last gift to us. We saw, ten paragraphs ago, that Oliver's data shows zero Fold mutation. Now Joana feels his cool skin and says *not like Su.* She doesn't yet know what the twitch is. We don't either. But the chapter's last gesture is to mark him as different. *Unnaturally cool* is the chapter's last hint.

There is a thread the book has been quietly pulling on for chapters. Læsa, in chapter 11, walked out of the Brisinger wreckage with no memory and increasingly translucent skin. She is — by every diagnostic that has touched her — *not human in the same way.*

The narrator subverted in the epigraph: *as it is above, so it is below.* The chapter spends three full plot threads showing that the surface and the depth are *not* exact mirrors. The colony's official broadcast and its hidden transcript are *not* the same speech. But they are in dialogue.

And then Oliver lies on a couch. The end of his stage of denial about his father's death. The isolation from his mother. And Earth. His mirror finally shattered. Like Ian had shattered his. The chapter ends inside the recursion.

Question for the listener. The narrator told you, at the chapter's start, that what survives the passage is *only what carries forward — code, stories, songs.* In this chapter we have a poem, a meme thread, a smuggled filter , a requisition for under-utilised capital hardware, and a sleeping boy. Which of those is the code that will outlast the colony?

The narrator told you you are *the next sentence.* If everything visible above is a transmission and everything happening below is the actual story — which of those are you reading? And which one is reading you?
